content:
Japanese has three writing systems. In hiragana, which you can use to write most Japanese words, each character represents a 
syllable
, and there are no spaces between words. We use romaji to represent Japanese sounds using the Roman alphabet:
すしとみず

Japanese does not have words like a or the. For example, the word 
せんせい
 [sensei] can mean both teacher and a teacher.

title: When you address someone, you use the title
content:
さん
 [san] after their name to show respect. When you use this with somebody's first name, it doesn't have a translation in English.
けんさん、こんにちは。

title: Using
content:
は
 [wa] after a word makes it the topic of the sentence. The rest of the sentence is a comment about it.
カレーはおいしいです。

You’ve seen that hiragana and katakana can be mixed in the same sentence. They can also be mixed within a single word! Compare the examples below:
これはアメリカです。

title: In Japanese, most question words come right before
content:
ですか
 [desu ka]. Compare the following questions:
ホテルはここですか。

title: To say something belongs to you, use the phrase
content:
わたしの
 [watashi no]:
これはわたしのかさです。

title: You’ve seen
content:
さん
 [san] used after a first name to show respect when addressing or describing someone else. It can also be used after a last name and translated as Mr. or Ms.
たなかさんはやさしいです。

The kanji writing system is used for most words in Japanese. There are over 2,000 kanji characters.  Kanji can really help you tell words apart! For example, notice the words that sound the same in the following sentences:
たなかさん、こんにちは。

title: To make a statement with
content:
です
 [desu] negative, use 
じゃないです
 [janai desu].
それはわたしのきっぷじゃないです。

title: Asking questions in Japanese is easy! Just add
content:
か
 (ka) to the end of a normal sentence, after the 
verb
!
Statement
Question
日本人です。

In this skill, you'll learn to say some numbers in Japanese!
いち
1
に
2
さん
3
よん
4

title: When you do something to a
content:
noun
, like when you eat something or drink something, put 
を
 (o) after the thing that receives the action.
Also, you may notice that 
verbs
 in Japanese often end with ‑
ます
 (‑masu). Notice that ‑
ます
 (‑masu) verbs can refer to either the present or the future, depending on context. It’s like when we say I’m eating now or I’m eating dinner later in English!

title: To ask What time is it? in Japanese, say
content:
何時ですか
 (nanji desu ka) To give an answer, just say the number and add 
時
 (ji)!

In Japanese, you just combine the numbers above to make bigger numbers. Take a look!
18
十八

title: To say what you want in Japanese, you can use the phrase
content:
欲しいです
. Be sure to put a 
が
 after the thing that you want!

In English we sometimes say things like do work, do yoga, and so on. Similarly, many Japanese verbs are made by adding 
します
 (shimasu) to 
nouns
.

title: We’ve seen a handful of hiragana characters like
content:
へ
 (e) and 
に
 (ni) (examples), which you place after a word to show what role it has in the sentence. Let’s look at two that are quite similar:
In short, 
は
 (wa) goes with a topic that’s been already mentioned or is implied, and 
が
 (ga) is used to give more attention to the subject in question.
田中さんは学生ですか？

title: You use words like
content:
いつも
 and 
よく
 to indicate how often you do something in Japanese.

title: We’ve seen
content:
です
 (desu) can mean to be, as in 
学生です
 (gakusei desu). But 
です
 (desu) is used in many other situations that aren’t translatable to English.
For example, to say I like water! or I like tea!, use 
が
  (ga) to mark the thing you like, then end with 
好きです
 (suki desu).
水が好きです。

In English, we’d say There is a table or There are tables. In Japanese, you can use the 
verb

title: それ
content:
(sore) and 
あれ
 (are) both mean that, so what’s the difference?
それ
 (sore) is to talk about things near the listener. (As opposed to 
これ
 (kore), which refers to things near the speaker.)
それは何ですか？

Where do you live? In English, we’d say I live in.... In Japanese, you end your sentence with the phrase ...
住んでいます

title: The kanji
content:
毎
 can attach to lots of words to mean every. 
毎日
every day
毎朝
every morning
毎晩
every night
毎年
every year
毎月
every month
毎週
every week

Japanese has lots of words to talk about family! When you talk to your family members, or when you talk about other people’s family, you generally use the words that end with that respectful 
‑さん
 (san).
おかえりなさい、お父さん。

title: Remember, when you count
content:
nouns
, you need to use a 
counter
 with the number. We’ve seen 
人
 in words like 
日本人
 (nihonjin). This kanji means person, and you also use it to count people. 
兄が一人います。

title: In English, we say on Monday. In Japanese, you put
content:
に
 after days of the week to show when you do something.

title: Use words like
content:
この
 (kono) before a 
noun
, like This food is great! Use words like 
これ
 (kore) when you don’t mention the noun, like This is great! 
この朝ご飯はおいしいです。

When saying what color something is in Japanese, we need to remember a few things! If the words end in 色 (which actually means color!), like 
オレンジ色
, put a 
の
 between the color and the thing!

title: When you use the word
content:
あまり
 with 
‑ません
, it shows that you don’t do something very much.  
彼女はあまり走りません。

In English, when we talk about how much time something takes, we usually use the words approximately or about before the time. In Japanese, you use the word 
くらい
 after the approximate time!
